title: Vascular Dementia
authors: Steven M. Greenberg, William W. Seeley

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.title: Understanding Vascular Contributions to Cognitive Impairment and Dementia (VCID)
items.content: The concept of VCID highlights the role of cerebrovascular disease in cognitive decline, often alongside other neurodegenerative processes. This broadened definition reflects the prevalence of vascular changes in the elderly and their contribution to cognitive impairment.
keyPoints: Vascular dementia is traditionally ranked as the second most frequent cause of dementia after Alzheimer's disease.
VCID encompasses the impact of cerebrovascular disease on age-related cognitive decline, often co-occurring with other neurodegenerative processes.
Multifactorial or 'mixed' dementias are more prevalent than single-etiology dementias.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · Vascular Dementia

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.title: Role of MRI in Detecting Vascular Contributions to Cognitive Decline
items.content: MRI scans reveal both symptomatic strokes and asymptomatic vascular lesions, which are significant contributors to cognitive impairment. These findings underscore the cumulative impact of small-vessel brain disease on cognitive function.
keyPoints: Approximately half of stroke survivors exhibit some level of cognitive impairment, increasing over time.
Individuals without symptomatic stroke but with MRI evidence of cerebrovascular disease have a substantially increased risk of cognitive impairment.
Postmortem studies show that vascular entities like infarcts and atherosclerosis contribute to ~20% of premortem cognitive decline.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · Vascular Dementia

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.title: Impact of Vascular Health on Dementia Incidence
items.content: Improving vascular health, particularly through blood pressure control, may reduce dementia incidence. Epidemiologic studies and trials like SPRINT-MIND suggest a decline in age-adjusted dementia rates correlates with better hypertension management.
keyPoints: Framingham Study reported declining 5-year age-and sex-adjusted cumulative hazard rates for dementia from 3.6 to 2.0 per 100 persons over several decades.

SPRINT-MIND trial showed reduced rates of mild cognitive impairment and combined mild cognitive impairment or probable dementia with lower systolic blood pressure targets (HR, 0.81; 95% CI, 0.69–0.95).
Evidence suggests a potential causative effect of blood pressure control on reducing all-cause cognitive impairment.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · Vascular Dementia

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.title: Variability in Vascular Dementia Across Populations
items.content: There is significant variability in the prevalence of vascular dementia across different ethnic groups, with intracranial atherosclerosis being more common in Asians, Hispanics, and American blacks compared to European and American whites. Conversely, extracranial disease may be more prevalent among whites.
keyPoints: Intracranial atherosclerosis is higher in Asians, Hispanics, and American blacks than in European and American whites.
Whites may have more extracranial cerebrovascular disease.
Disparities likely involve healthcare access, lifestyle factors such as diet, and genetic influences.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.title: Poststroke Cognitive Decline Dynamics
items.content: Stroke patients often experience accelerated cognitive decline post-recovery, with a shift from average prestroke cognitive gain to poststroke cognitive loss. This is attributed to ongoing cerebrovascular disease and reduced cognitive reserve.
keyPoints: Prestroke cognitive gain: 0.021 points/year.
Poststroke cognitive loss: -0.035 points/year on the six-item screener global cognitive function scale.
Mechanisms include ongoing effects of cerebrovascular disease and loss of cognitive reserve.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.title: Distinguishing Small-Vessel Diseases by MRI
items.content: Arteriolosclerosis and cerebral amyloid angiopathy, two common small-vessel diseases, can often be distinguished radiographically. Arteriolosclerosis affects deep penetrating territories, while cerebral amyloid angiopathy affects superficial lobar regions.
keyPoints: Arteriolosclerosis: thickening of arterioles due to plasma proteins, risk factors include age, hypertension, diabetes mellitus.
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy: deposition of $\beta$-amyloid peptide in small vessels, primary risk factor is advancing age.
MRI characteristics: lacunar infarcts (3–15 mm) and microbleeds distinguish arteriolosclerosis; lobar hemorrhages indicate cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)

items.subtopic: SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Binswanger's Disease and Its Neuroimaging Features
items.content: Binswanger's disease, also known as subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy, is characterized by severe diffuse white matter vascular injury. It manifests with gradual cognitive deterioration and notable changes in small-vessel ischemic disease on neuroimaging. The imaging features include progressive confluent subcortical and periventricular white matter disease, along with hypoperfusion and hypometabolism.
keyPoints: Binswanger's disease is recognized by gradual cognitive deterioration and notable white matter changes due to small-vessel ischemic disease.
Neuroimaging shows progressive confluent subcortical and periventricular white matter disease, with associated hypoperfusion and hypometabolism.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)

items.subtopic: SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)
items.title: Role of Diffusion Tensor MRI in Assessing White Matter Integrity
items.content: Diffusion tensor MRI provides a sensitive and quantitative assessment of white matter structure, detecting subtle alterations through increased water diffusivity or decreased diffusion directionality. These measures are consistently associated with cognitive performance and gait speed, highlighting the importance of brain network disconnection in cerebral small-vessel disease.
keyPoints: Diffusion tensor MRI detects subtle changes in white matter structure by measuring increased water diffusivity or decreased diffusion directionality.
These measures correlate with cognitive performance and gait speed, indicating the impact of brain network disconnection.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)

items.question: What is traditionally considered the second most frequent cause of dementia?
items.explanation: Vascular dementia is usually ranked as the second most frequent cause of dementia, after Alzheimer's disease.

items.question: Which imaging modality is commonly used to detect asymptomatic vascular lesions contributing to cognitive impairment?
items.explanation: Brain MRI scans are commonly used to detect asymptomatic vascular lesions contributing to cognitive impairment.

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.question: What was the systolic blood pressure target in the SPRINT-MIND trial for reducing rates of mild cognitive impairment?
items.explanation: The SPRINT-MIND trial targeted a systolic blood pressure of <120 mmHg.

items.question: Which population groups have been found to have a higher prevalence of intracranial atherosclerosis compared to European and American whites?
items.explanation: Intracranial atherosclerosis is higher in Asians, Hispanics, and American blacks than it is in European and American whites.

items.question: What are the primary risk factors for arteriolosclerosis?
items.explanation: The primary risk factors for arteriolosclerosis are age, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus.

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.question: Which cerebral small-vessel disease is characterized by the deposition of $\beta$-amyloid peptide in vessel walls?
items.explanation: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is defined by the deposition of $\beta$-amyloid peptide in small cerebral arteries, arterioles, and capillaries.

items.question: Where are cerebral microbleeds most commonly located if caused by arteriolosclerosis?
items.explanation: Cerebral microbleeds are primarily located in deep penetrating brain regions if caused by arteriolosclerosis.

items.question: What does Binswanger's disease refer to?

Extremely severe diffuse white matter vascular injury
An MRI marker for cerebral amyloid angiopathy
items.explanation: Binswanger's disease refers to extremely severe diffuse white matter vascular injury.

items.subtopic: SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)
items.question: What is the role of functional MRI in clinical practice for cerebrovascular reactivity?
options: It is routinely performed to assess cognitive decline
It is typically used to identify structural brain injury
It is generally not performed but may become abnormal before structural changes
It is used to diagnose Alzheimer's disease
items.explanation: Functional MRI measurement of cerebrovascular reactivity is generally not performed in clinical practice.

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.statement: Population-based studies show no increased risk of cognitive impairment among individuals without symptomatic stroke but with MRI evidence of cerebrovascular disease.
items.explanation: Studies demonstrate a substantially increased risk of cognitive impairment in individuals with MRI evidence of cerebrovascular disease, even without symptomatic stroke.

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.statement: The Framingham Study reported an increase in age-and sex-adjusted cumulative hazard rates for dementia over time.
items.explanation: The Framingham Study reported a decline in age-and sex-adjusted cumulative hazard rates for dementia over the observed periods.

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.statement: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is most often recognized symptomatically as a cause of ischemic stroke.
items.explanation: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is most commonly associated with intracerebral hemorrhage, not ischemic stroke.

items.statement: Stroke patients who recover cognitively often experience accelerated cognitive decline post-stroke.
items.explanation: Even stroke patients with good cognitive recovery show an accelerated poststroke cognitive decline.

items.subtopic: SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)
items.statement: White matter hyperintensities on T2-weighted MRI are specific markers of white matter gliosis.
items.explanation: White matter hyperintensities represent a nonspecific marker.

items.statement: The co-occurrence of cerebrovascular and neurodegenerative lesions can lead to greater cognitive impairment than each disease alone.
items.explanation: The combination produces more impairment than expected from each mechanism individually.

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.assertion: Improving vascular health may have a public impact on reducing dementia incidence.
items.reason: Epidemiologic evidence shows a decline in age-adjusted dementia incidence coinciding with improved hypertension control and stroke prevention.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · Vascular Dementia

items.subtopic: Vascular Dementia
items.assertion: The SPRINT-MIND trial demonstrated that reducing systolic blood pressure to <120 mmHg significantly reduced rates of dementia alone.
items.reason: The study showed a hazard ratio for dementia alone as 0.83 (95% CI, 0.67–1.04).
items.explanation: While the assertion is false because the reduction in systolic blood pressure did not significantly reduce rates of dementia alone, the reason correctly states the hazard ratio.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · Vascular Dementia

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.assertion: The causes of disparities in vascular dementia prevalence include lifestyle factors such as diet.
items.reason: Access to health care, lifestyle factors like diet, and genetic influences are likely contributors to these disparities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)
items.assertion: Microinfarcts are less numerous than lacunar infarcts.
items.reason: Microinfarcts are substantially more numerous but less easily visualized compared to lacunar infarcts.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because microinfarcts are actually more numerous than lacunar infarcts.
items.reference: Vascular Dementia · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 1)

items.subtopic: SUBTYPES OF CEREBROVASCULAR DISEASE ASSOCIATED WITH VCID (part 2)
items.assertion: Diffusion tensor MRI measures are consistently associated with cognitive performance.
items.reason: They reflect the disconnection of key brain networks in cerebral small-vessel disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
